diagrams.title: HTTP Request-Response Lifecycle with Jakarta Servlets
diagrams.description: Shows how an HTTP request flows from client browser through Tomcat server to servlet and back
elements: Client Browser (sends HTTP request with method, headers, parameters)
Server sends HTTP response back to client
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete flow of HTTP communication in servlet-based web applications and how the Jakarta servlet.http package components interact

diagrams.title: HttpServletRequest Object Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the structure and key methods of HttpServletRequest interface
elements: Request Line: getRequestURI(), getRequestURL(), getMethod(), getProtocol()
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to access all HTTP request information using HttpServletRequest methods for building dynamic web applications

diagrams.title: HttpServletResponse Object Structure
diagrams.description: Shows HttpServletResponse components and methods for building HTTP responses
elements: Status Codes: setStatus(), sendError(), SC_OK, SC_NOT_FOUND, SC_INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how to construct proper HTTP responses including status codes, headers, cookies, and content using HttpServletResponse methods

diagrams.title: Session Management Flow with HttpSession
diagrams.description: Diagram showing how HttpSession tracks user state across multiple requests
elements: First Request: Client sends request without session ID
Server creates new HttpSession and generates unique Session ID
Server sends Set-Cookie header with JSESSIONID to client
Subsequent Requests: Client sends Cookie with JSESSIONID
Server validates session ID and retrieves existing HttpSession
Session attributes can be stored/retrieved (user preferences, login status, shopping cart)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn session tracking mechanism using HttpSession for maintaining user state in web applications like login systems and shopping carts

diagrams.title: Cookie Handling Flow Diagram
diagrams.description: Shows how cookies are created, sent to client, and read back in subsequent requests
elements: Servlet creates Cookie object with name and value
Response.addCookie(cookie) sets Set-Cookie header in HTTP response
Later request includes Cookie header with stored cookies
Cookie methods: getName(), getValue(), setMaxAge(), setPath(), getDomain()
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete cookie lifecycle for implementing features like remember-me, user preferences, and tracking

charts.title: HttpServletRequest Key Methods Comparison
charts.content:
| Method Category | Methods | Purpose |
|-----------------|---------|----------|
| Request Info | getMethod(), getRequestURI(), getProtocol() | Get HTTP method, URI, protocol version |
| Headers | getHeader(), getHeaders(), getHeaderNames() | Access HTTP request headers |
| Parameters | getParameter(), getParameterValues(), getParameterMap() | Get form/query parameters |
| Attributes | getAttribute(), setAttribute(), removeAttribute() | Request-scoped data storage |
| Session | getSession(), getRequestedSessionId() | Session management |
| Cookies | getCookies() | Retrieve cookies from request |
| Input | getInputStream(), getReader() | Read request body |

charts.title: HttpServletResponse Key Methods Comparison
charts.content:
| Method Category | Methods | Purpose |
|-----------------|---------|----------|
| Status | setStatus(), sendError(), sendRedirect() | Set HTTP status codes and redirects |
| Headers | setHeader(), addHeader(), setDateHeader() | Set response headers |
| Content Type | setContentType(), setCharacterEncoding() | Specify response format |
| Output | getOutputStream(), getWriter() | Write response body |
| Cookies | addCookie() | Send cookies to client |
| Buffering | setBufferSize(), reset() | Manage response buffer |

charts.title: doGet vs doPost Method Dispatch Flow
charts.content:
Client Request → Tomcat Container → Servlet.service() → Method Dispatch:
- GET request → doGet(HttpServletRequest, HttpServletResponse)
- POST request → doPost(HttpServletRequest, HttpServletResponse)
- HEAD request → doHead()
- PUT request → doPut()
- DELETE request → doDelete()
Each method handles specific HTTP operation and returns appropriate response to client

charts.title: HttpSession vs Cookies Comparison
charts.content:
| Feature | HttpSession | Cookies |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| Storage Location | Server-side (memory/file/database) | Client-side (browser) |
| Capacity | Unlimited (server resources) | ~4KB per cookie, ~20 cookies per domain |
| Security | More secure (data stays on server) | Less secure (data visible to client) |
| Expiry | Session timeout or manual invalidation | Max-Age orExpires set in cookie |
| Use Cases | Login sessions, shopping carts | User preferences, remember me, tracking |
| Performance | Slightly slower (server lookup) | Faster (no server storage) |

branches: HttpServlet - Abstract class for HTTP servlets
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview showing all major classes and interfaces in the Jakarta servlet.http package and their relationships for handling HTTP-based web applications
